text: In this video, we present the case of a patient with adenocarcinoma of the pancreas and liver metastases located in the right liver.
A 3D reconstruction displays simulations of pancreatic adenocarcinoma and liver metastases, with both tumors highlighted in pink.
The 3D reconstruction displays simulations of the pancreatic adenocarcinoma and liver metastases, both highlighted in pink.
In this picture, the relative position of the patient, the surgeons and the surgical robot is represented.
The image shows the relative positioning of the patient, surgeons, and the surgical robot within the operating room setup.
The surgeon performs an extra-glycemonian approach to the right hilar pedicles, utilizing techniques developed by Takasaki and Sojioka.
The surgeon performs an extra-glycemonian approach to the right hilar pedicles, adhering to the techniques established by Takasaki and Sojioka.
The surgeon initiates the procedure with a cholecystectomy, exposing the posterior section plane of the liver parenchyma and identifying the anterior and posterior right hilar pedicles.
The surgeon initiates the procedure with a cholecystectomy, exposing the posterior section plane of the liver parenchyma and identifying the anterior and posterior right hilar pedicles.
As in laparoscopic surgery, in robotic surgery the Pringle maneuver is mandatory and must be prepared to prevent and manage bleeding problems during the course of surgery.

As in laparoscopic surgery, in robotic surgery the Pringle maneuver is mandatory and must be prepared to prevent and manage bleeding problems during the course of surgery. The use of the Wong loop is very useful and very easy to do with robotic devices.
The surgeon utilizes a Wong loop technique with robotic devices, employing a urinary catheter to facilitate the procedure.
To do this, we use a urinary catheter.

The surgeon identifies the right anterior pedicle by separating the lay neck capsule using blunt maneuvers at gates four and five. The pedicle is then surrounded with a tape, preparing it for clamping to mark its exact extension.

The surgeon identifies the right anterior pedicle by separating the lay neck capsule using blunt maneuvers at gates four and five. The pedicle is surrounded with a tape, preparing it for clamping to mark its exact extension.
The surgeon identifies the right posterior hilar pedicle and its corresponding gate, surrounding it with a tape for subsequent clamping.
The surgeon identifies the right posterior hilar pedicle and its corresponding gate, surrounding the pedicle with a tape before clamping it.
The surgeon visualizes the demarcation of the ischemia line on the liver surface, indicating areas affected by reduced blood flow.
The surgeon visualizes the demarcation of the ischemia line, indicating areas of reduced blood flow in the surgical field.

The surgical robot displays a coupled image of the intraoperative ultrasound, showing the middle hepatic vein and subsidiary veins of hepatic segments five and eight. The surgeon begins partitioning the liver parenchyma.

The surgical robot displays a coupled image of the intraoperative ultrasound within the same visual field, allowing identification of the middle hepatic vein and subsidiary veins of hepatic segments five and eight.

The surgeon begins partitioning the liver parenchyma using robotic instruments that combine parenchyma clasia and high-energy sealing. Maryland bipolar forceps are utilized for precise dissection of vascular and biliary structures. Polymer clips are applied as needed during the progression of the parenchyma partition.

Robotic instruments are utilized to combine parenchyma clasia and high-energy sealing techniques. The Maryland bipolar forceps are employed for precise dissection of vascular and biliary structures. As the partition of the liver parenchyma progresses, polymer clips are applied to secure vessels.

The surgeon identifies the vein of hepatic segment V, which is clipped and sectioned. Subsequently, the veins of hepatic segment VIII are identified and carefully sectioned, taking special care to prevent opening.

The surgeon identifies the vein of hepatic segment V, which is clipped and sectioned. Subsequently, the veins of segment VIII are identified and carefully sectioned, taking special care to prevent opening.

The surgeon uses a robotic endo stapler with a triple staple line to section the right anterior and posterior hilar pedicles, adjusting the staple size to 3.5 millimeters to match the tissue thickness. The maneuvers are performed with a short prangle for precision.

The surgeon uses a robotic endo stapler with a triple staple line to section the right anterior and posterior hilar pedicles, adjusting the staple size to 3.5 millimeters to match the tissue thickness. The maneuvers are performed with a short prangle for precision.

The surgeon dissects along the anterior wall of the vena cava, reaching the entrance of the right hepatic vein. The right hepatic vein is encircled with a tape and then sectioned using a robotic endo stapler with 2.5 mm staples.

The surgeon dissects along the anterior wall of the vena cava, reaching the entrance of the right hepatic vein. The right hepatic vein is encircled with a tape and then sectioned using a robotic endo stapler with 2.5 mm staples.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to free the right liver from its adhesions to the diaphragm, carefully dividing the small veins that drain directly into the vena cava.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to free the right liver from its adhesions to the diaphragm, carefully dividing the small veins that drain directly into the vena cava.
The surgeon frees the inferior part of the pancreas from its adhesions, identifying the splenic vein and artery during the distal pancreatectomy.
The surgeon performs a distal pancreatectomy by freeing the inferior part of the pancreas from its adhesions, while identifying the splenic vein and artery.
The surgeon divides the splenic vessels using polymer clips and a robotic endostapler, ensuring secure closure of the vessels during the procedure.
The surgeon divides the splenic vessels using polymer clips and a robotic endostapler, securing the vessels before sectioning them.

The surgeon incises the pancreatic parenchyma at the junction of the body and tail using an endostapler. The excised surgical specimens are placed into plastic bags for removal from the surgical field.
